findings.title: `/new` context reset is tracked globally, so queued follow-up messages can miss or steal the reset
findings.description: The new implementation no longer tears down the session on `/new`; instead it only inserts the thread into `needs_context` after queuing `/new`. That flag is consumed only on the direct-execution path. If a session is busy and the user sends `/new` followed immediately by another message, the follow-up is enqueued as a mid-turn inject without any context injection, and the shared `needs_context` flag remains until some later direct turn consumes it. In practice, the first post-reset message can run with the wrong context, or an unrelated later message can receive the reset banner instead.
findings.suggestion: Bind the reset state to the queued message sequence instead of a shared thread-level flag, or preserve the old hard reset semantics. At minimum, ensure the first message after `/new` is transformed when it is dequeued, not only when a later direct turn starts.

findings.title: Rate-limit telemetry is now dropped instead of updating the shared monitor state
findings.description: This patch removes all `ratelimit_tx.send_modify(...)` updates from the worker and now explicitly skips `StreamEvent::RateLimit`. `main.rs` still starts a rate-limit monitor from that watch channel for Discord presence and alerts, so after this change the shared `RateLimitInfo` stays stale/default forever. That is a functional regression in the bot's rate-limit handling and can hide approaching limits from operators.
findings.suggestion: Keep propagating `StreamEvent::RateLimit` into the shared watch state in every path where those events are read, or remove the monitor entirely in the same PR if that behavior is intentionally being deleted.

findings.title: Progress messages are no longer cleaned up, which can spam Discord during long turns
findings.description: `finalize()` now edits the progress message to a completed state and then drops the tracked `message_id`, while `cleanup()` simply calls `finalize()` again instead of deleting the status message. Because `on_tool_use()` and `on_event()` finalize the current indicator before starting a new one, a long turn with several tools leaves behind a new `-# ⏱️ ...` message for each phase. That is a channel-noise regression and increases message/edit volume against Discord rate limits.
findings.suggestion: Delete or reuse a single progress message for the entire turn. If a final status is required, emit at most one terminal update at turn end rather than one per tool/thinking segment.

summary: The patch has three notable regressions: `/new` reset sequencing can apply to the wrong message, rate-limit telemetry is effectively disconnected, and the new progress indicator can leave behind many extra Discord messages during long turns.
